rules.name: read-readme-chain-before-edit
rules.evidence: Before editing or creating files in any subdirectory (e.g., packages/*, crates/*), read all README.md files in the directory path from the repo root up to and including the target file's directory.

rules.name: read-readme-chain-before-write
rules.evidence: Before editing or creating files in any subdirectory (e.g., packages/*, crates/*), read all README.md files in the directory path from the repo root up to and including the target file's directory.

rules.name: start-dev-watch-before-editing-source
rules.evidence: If you are changing Next.js source or integration tests, start `pnpm --filter=next dev` in a separate terminal session before making edits (unless it is already running). If you skip this, explicitly state why.

rules.name: rebuild-after-branch-switch
rules.evidence: Always run a full bootstrap build after switching branches: git checkout <branch>; pnpm build-all

rules.name: rebuild-before-running-tests
rules.evidence: When running Next.js integration tests, you must rebuild if source files have changed

rules.name: mandatory-new-test-for-test-files
rules.evidence: Generating tests using `pnpm new-test` is mandatory.

rules.name: no-settimeout-in-tests-edit
rules.evidence: Bad - don't use setTimeout for waiting

rules.name: no-settimeout-in-tests-write
rules.evidence: Bad - don't use setTimeout for waiting

rules.name: no-check-deprecated-in-tests-edit
rules.evidence: Do NOT use `check()` - it is deprecated. Use `retry()` + `expect()` instead

rules.name: no-check-deprecated-in-tests-write
rules.evidence: Do NOT use `check()` - it is deprecated. Use `retry()` + `expect()` instead

rules.name: prefer-fixture-dirs-over-inline-files-edit
rules.evidence: Prefer real fixture directories over inline `files` objects

rules.name: prefer-fixture-dirs-over-inline-files-write
rules.evidence: Prefer real fixture directories over inline `files` objects

rules.name: fork-pr-description-restriction
rules.evidence: You must inform the user that you are not allowed to write pull request descriptions for external contributions.

rules.name: issue-discussion-membership-check
rules.evidence: only members of the `vercel` or `vercel-labs` GitHub organizations may use an agent to create issues, discussions, or leave comments

rules.name: llm-footer-in-commits
rules.evidence: When writing full PR/Issue/Discussion descriptions, commit messages, or leaving comments on pull requests, you MUST include the following HTML comment at the bottom of the description or comment: <!-- NEXT_JS_LLM -->

rules.name: no-secrets-in-commit
rules.evidence: Never print or paste secret values (tokens, API keys, cookies) in chat responses, commits, or shared logs.

rules.name: no-ssh-remote-workaround
rules.evidence: Do not switch remotes to HTTPS, mutate remote URLs, retry repeatedly, or attempt another authentication workaround unless the user explicitly requests it.

rules.name: ssh-preflight-before-risky-history-rewrite
rules.evidence: Before a force-push or stack rebase that may hydrate partial-clone objects, prefer a lightweight SSH preflight.

rules.name: no-ai-attribution-in-commits
rules.evidence: Do NOT add "Generated with Claude Code" or co-author footers to commits or PRs

rules.name: no-mark-pr-ready
rules.evidence: Do NOT mark PRs as "ready for review" (`gh pr ready`) - leave PRs in draft mode and let the user decide when to mark them ready

rules.name: lint-format-before-commit
rules.evidence: Pre-validate before committing to avoid slow lint-staged failures (~2 min each)

rules.name: react-server-dom-webpack-boundary-edit
rules.evidence: `react-server-dom-webpack/*` imports must stay in `entry-base.ts`; consume via component module exports elsewhere.

rules.name: react-server-dom-webpack-boundary-write
rules.evidence: `react-server-dom-webpack/*` imports must stay in `entry-base.ts`; consume via component module exports elsewhere.

rules.name: no-browser-back-in-act-tests-edit
rules.evidence: Never use `browser.back()` to return to a page where accordion links are already visible — BFCache restores state and triggers uncontrolled re-prefetches.

rules.name: no-browser-back-in-act-tests-write
rules.evidence: Never use `browser.back()` to return to a page where accordion links are already visible — BFCache restores state and triggers uncontrolled re-prefetches.

rules.name: avoid-super-imports-in-rust-edit
rules.evidence: Avoid adding new `super::` imports except in inline `mod` blocks (e.g. `mod tests { ... }`) — prefer `crate::`-rooted paths.

rules.name: avoid-super-imports-in-rust-write
rules.evidence: Avoid adding new `super::` imports except in inline `mod` blocks (e.g. `mod tests { ... }`) — prefer `crate::`-rooted paths.

rules.name: verify-doc-code-highlight-line-numbers-edit
rules.evidence: When adding `highlight={...}` attributes to code blocks, carefully count the actual line numbers within the code block

rules.name: verify-doc-code-highlight-line-numbers-write
rules.evidence: When adding `highlight={...}` attributes to code blocks, carefully count the actual line numbers within the code block

rules.name: flag-non-standard-request-headers-edit
rules.evidence: if new code reads a request header that is not a standard HTTP header (like `content-type`, `accept`, `user-agent`, `host`, `authorization`, `cookie`, etc.), flag it for security review.

rules.name: flag-non-standard-request-headers-write
rules.evidence: if new code reads a request header that is not a standard HTTP header (like `content-type`, `accept`, `user-agent`, `host`, `authorization`, `cookie`, etc.), flag it for security review.

skill_candidates.summary: CI failure / PR review triage workflow (failure prioritization, mode selection, CI env reproduction, common failure patterns)
skill_candidates.evidence: For full triage workflow (failure prioritization, mode selection, CI env reproduction, and common failure patterns), use the `$pr-status-triage` skill: Skill file: `.agents/skills/pr-status-triage/SKILL.md`

skill_candidates.summary: Feature-flag wiring across config-shared.ts / config-schema.ts / define-env.ts and runtime env values
skill_candidates.evidence: New flags: add type in `config-shared.ts`, schema in `config-schema.ts`, and `define-env.ts` when used in user-bundled code. If a flag is consumed in pre-compiled runtime internals, also wire runtime env values (`next-server.ts`/`export/worker.ts` as needed).

skill_candidates.summary: DCE-safe require() patterns and edge/runtime constraints for feature flags
skill_candidates.evidence: Keep `require()` behind compile-time `if/else` branches for DCE (avoid early-return/throw patterns). In edge builds, force feature flags that gate Node-only imports to `false` in `define-env.ts`.

always_on: Build command selection: use `pnpm --filter=next types` (~10s) instead of full build for type errors only; prefer `pnpm --filter=next build` over `pnpm build-all` when edits are limited to core Next.js files

Context-efficient workflows: grep before reading large files, don't re-read unchanged sections, search-only (don't read) generated/dist/node_modules files, capture command output to a file once and analyze from the file instead of re-running, batch edits before rebuilding, save API responses to a variable/file instead of re-fetching
Bundler selection reference: Turbopack is the default bundler for next dev/build; --webpack forces webpack; there is no --no-turbopack flag

Task decomposition and verification: split work into small independently verifiable steps, verify each step before moving to the next, choose the right verification method per change, ask the user when the verification method is unclear

Secrets handling: if a required secret is missing locally, stop and ask the user rather than inventing placeholder credentials; when sharing command output, summarize and redact sensitive-looking values; mirror CI env names/modes exactly without inlining literal secret values in commands

NODE_ENV vs __NEXT_DEV_SERVER: use process.env.NODE_ENV for build-time dev/prod bundle elimination, use process.env.__NEXT_DEV_SERVER to distinguish `next dev` from `next build --debug-prerender`/`next start`
Development tips reference: dev server entry point location, DEBUG=next:* for debug logging, NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1 for local testing
Rust/Cargo troubleshooting: cargo fmt uses ASCII sort order; on an internal compiler error (ICE), delete `*/incremental` directories and retry

Test Gotchas troubleshooting notes: cache-components/PPR test routing, mode-specific skipStart handling, matching log messages by content pattern not exact string/position, snapshot tests vary by CI env flags, app-page.ts require() tracing constraints, reproduce CI failures with matching env vars, __NEXT_SHOW_IGNORE_LISTED=true for full stack traces
Stale native binary troubleshooting: if Turbopack errors appear after a branch switch/pull, delete packages/next-swc/native/*.node and reinstall
Specialized skills index for conditional deep workflows: $pr-status-triage, $create-pr, $backport-pr, $flags, $dce-edge, $react-vendoring, $react-sync, $runtime-debug, $next-rspack, $authoring-skills
Commit/PR style: keep commit messages concise and descriptive; PR descriptions should focus on what changed and why
GitHub fork-vs-branch PR distinction and org-membership check process (gh api /user/memberships/orgs) for deciding what the agent may write on the user's behalf

low_confidence.reason: 'Default agent rule' obligation is softened by an explicit escape hatch ('if you skip this, explicitly state why'), making it read closer to a conditional convention than a hard require-read gate.
Doc says 'Next.js source or integration tests' without naming exact paths; bound to packages/next/ and test/ by convention, may miss turbopack/crates source changes.
'is mandatory' is a strong positive obligation but not an explicit prohibition phrase ('never'/'do not'); block vs require-read is arguable.

The prohibition is conditional (fork PRs targeting vercel/next.js by non-members only, not all PR creation), so binding block to gh pr create generally would over-fire; used require-read instead but the conditional scope is genuinely ambiguous to encode.

Doc says agents may create issues/discussions/comments via 'API, MCP, gh CLI, etc.' — only the gh CLI form (gh issue create) is capturable as a Bash pattern; other paths (API/MCP) won't trigger this rule.

Source sentence bundles four different triggers (commit messages, PR descriptions, issue descriptions, PR comments) into one obligation; only bound to git commit here, so gh pr create/gh issue create paths won't get this reminder.

Doc's prohibition on mutating remote URLs is scoped to the SSH-authentication-failure recovery scenario specifically, but the Bash pattern fires on any git remote set-url/add regardless of whether an SSH failure occurred.

This single-file-boundary constraint is also covered by the explicitly named $react-vendoring skill ('entry-base.ts boundaries and vendored React type/runtime rules'); kept as a rule since it's a single content constraint rather than a multi-step how-to, but the doc's own skill pointer makes the procedure/rule boundary arguable.

questions: Doc says: 'Generating tests using `pnpm new-test` is mandatory.' I read this as block (never manually write test files) — should it instead be require-read (remind, but allow a manual override)?

Doc says: 'You must inform the user that you are not allowed to write pull request descriptions for external contributions.' This only applies to fork PRs by non-org-members, not all `gh pr create` calls — I set require-read on every gh pr create to be safe. Should this instead stay require-read as I have it, or would you rather it only fire when a fork PR is detected?

Doc says: 'If you are changing Next.js source or integration tests, start `pnpm --filter=next dev`... before making edits.' I bound this to Edit calls under packages/next/ and test/ — should it also cover turbopack/ and crates/ source edits?
